dialogue.utterance: Mr. George, I'm Alex from EMS. We're here because you called about breathing issues and constipation.
Can you tell me more about the breathing problems and constipation you mentioned?
I was having a bit of shortness of breath this morning but not now. The constipation started recently, and I'm just not going as regularly as before.
Have you experienced any chest pain or discomfort at all with the breathing issues?
No, no chest pain. It just felt like a wave of breathlessness that went away.
Mr. George, can you tell me the date and who I am?
You're Alex, and I think we're in [CITY]. Today must be Thursday.
Can I ask you to squeeze my fingers to test motor response?
Sure, here you go.
Your airway is clear and patent with no signs of obstruction.
Your breathing is unlabored and regular. It was 20 breaths per minute earlier, now at 16.
Pulse is strong and regular - 75 at first, now 77. Skin is warm and dry.
Mr. George, you said the shortness of breath was sudden but has now resolved. Was there any chest pressure or tightness with it?
No, it was just a wave, and I stayed alert the whole time.
When did the constipation start, and have you noticed any other changes recently?
A couple of days ago. No real changes. I can still pass gas, but not on a regular schedule.
Your medical record shows a history of stroke, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Is that correct?
Yes. The left side weakness from my stroke is still there.
Do you have known allergies to food, medications, or the environment?
No known allergies, both drug and environmental.
Your pulse was 75 at first and now 77 - remains consistent and regular.
Breathing stable, from 20 to 16 without signs of distress.
Blood pressure was 136/71, now 126/85. Still within normal for you.
Oxygen saturation measured between 96% and 95% - normal.
We attempted glucose reading, but a device wasn't available at the time.
ECG shows sinus rhythm with moderate left axis deviation for a 40+ male.
Skin is intact with no evidence of rashes, bruises, or abnormal dryness.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. No neck pain or swelling reported.
Ears, nose, and mouth are unremarkable. No obstruction or discharge noted.
Chest is free of trauma, with equal, clear bilateral breath sounds.
Do you currently have any weakness or numbness on your left side, as expected from your stroke?
Yes, but just on the left side as with my stroke. Otherwise, no new symptoms.
Your abdomen is soft, non-tender, and no distension. You have constipation but no pain.
All extremities are mobile. Left leg and arm remain weak due to the stroke but no new issues noted.
We attempted to start an IV using a 20G needle but were unsuccessful.
Reassessing now: BP 126/85, respirations 16, no signs of acute distress.
Oxygen levels remain steady between 95% and 96%. Vital signs unchanged.
Do you experience any new symptoms or discomfort at the moment?
No, I remained alert the whole time and have no pain.
Constipation remains unchanged and no history of new medications or drug use.
We are ready to transport to John Willis Medical Center. Any concerns before we proceed?
Mr. George, we're going to secure you for the transfer to the hospital.
You will be placed on the stretcher and secured using a 5-point harness.
You are alert and oriented with no signs of new neurological symptoms.
No gross deformities in extremities or other serious signs identified.
We'll safely move you to the stretcher now.
Your medical history of stroke, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia will be detailed in the care report.
Mr. George is a 76-year-old male with alert mental status and no deficiencies.
Pulse 75 to 77, respirations 20 to 16, BP range 136/71 to 126/85, O2 between 96% and 95%.
ECG showed borderline left axis deviation in a middle-aged or older male, sinus rhythm, no acute cardiac compromise noted.
IV line start was unsuccessful on one attempt.
No pain reported at any time during examination or reassessment.
Patient is responsive and remains in no acute distress with no changes from our initial assessment.
Constipation without abdominal pain, tenderness, or distension noted.
No known drug or environmental allergies. His history includes no known infarction but does include stroke-related CVA.
Left-sided weakness from stroke is present, no new neurological deficits observed.
Patient denied chest pain, dyspnea, or weight gain and showed no signs of ACS.
Mr. George is now securely positioned for transport with no concerns raised by EMS team for transport.
Patient is ready to be transferred to the emergency room at John Willis Medical Center.
ER team, receiving an alert, oriented 76-year-old male with the documented conditions.
He remains asymptomatic and with no emergent conditions during transport period.
All forms completed, care report shared, and items transferred to the ER.
Handover complete and confirmed with the emergency room.
EMS team will now return to base upon completion of the transfer.
All duties completed. Move out confirmed.
Call completed and transfer of care documented under protocol 3-11.
Mr. George appeared stable and without acute health concerns at the time of transfer.
